# /gcal-birthday-sync

Sync birthdays from contacts to calendar.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Birthdays live in Google Contacts but never made it onto the calendar the user actually looks at - so they keep finding out on the day, from social media. This skill reads birthday fields from Contacts, checks what already exists on the calendar, and proposes recurring yearly all-day events (optionally with a heads-up reminder a few days before for gift-buying).

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; only ask if missing:

- Scope: all contacts with a birthday, or only a group/label (e.g. "Family", "Friends", starred)
- Target calendar: primary, or a dedicated "Birthdays" calendar (recommended so it can be toggled off)
- Reminder rule (default: notification on the day at 9:00 + 3 days before for close contacts)
- How to handle birthdays with no year recorded (they're common - event title without age)

## Tone & voice

- **Tone:** Time-zone aware. Propose calendar changes before applying.
- **Audience:** Infer from user context.

## Connector access (required)

This skill **cannot run from text alone** - it must call live integrations.
Before any workflow step:

1. Check your AI tool's connector settings and confirm each integration below is enabled and authenticated (Grok: run `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: enable the app in settings · other tools: ask your AI what app connections it has).
2. Use `search_tool` to discover tools for each connector (search by app name).
3. Call integrations with `use_tool` and fully-qualified names (`server__tool_name`).
4. Connector tool names vary by AI tool. Discover the available tools first, then call the exact tool name exposed in the current session.

### Required integrations

- **@google-calendar** - Google Calendar
  - MCP server key: `google-calendar`
  - Example tools: `google-calendar__list_events`, `google-calendar__create_event`, `google-calendar__suggest_time`

- **@google-contacts** - Google Contacts
  - MCP server key: `google-people`
  - Example tools: `google-people__search_contacts`, `google-people__get_contact`

### If a connector is missing

Stop immediately. Tell the user:

```
This skill needs <@mention> enabled.
1. Open your AI tool's connector/integration settings (Grok: `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: app settings)
2. Enable and authenticate <app>
3. Refresh your tool list, then run this skill again
```

Do **not** pretend you accessed Gmail/Canva/Drive without a successful `use_tool` call.

## Safety

- **Propose before write**: show a review table; wait for user to say `apply`, `apply all`, or `apply 1,3`.
- Never delete, send, or publish without explicit confirmation.
- Summarize every action taken at the end of the run so the user can review it.

## Workflow

1. Discover the Contacts and Calendar tools in this session. Pull the in-scope contacts and keep only those with a birthday field populated; record name, month/day, and year-if-present for each.
2. Check what already exists before proposing anything:
   - Google's built-in "Birthdays" calendar may already show contact birthdays - if the user has it enabled, warn that syncing to another calendar creates duplicates and ask which source should win.
   - Search the target calendar for existing events matching "<name>*birthday" to catch manually created ones.
3. Build the proposal per contact:
   - All-day event "🎂 <Name>'s birthday", recurring yearly (RRULE `FREQ=YEARLY`).
   - **Feb 29 birthdays**: propose Feb 28 in non-leap years (or Mar 1 - ask once, apply consistently).
   - No birth year recorded → plain title; year recorded → optionally "(turns 40)" only if the user opts in - age display is a privacy choice.
   - Reminders per the agreed rule; longer lead time for the close-contacts group.
4. Classify each row ADD (nothing exists) / SKIP (already present and correct) / FIX (exists but wrong date or not recurring).
5. Present the review table; write only after `apply`, `apply all`, or `apply 1,3`. Create events with **no attendees** - never invite the birthday person to their own reminder.
6. Report created/updated events and suggest the monthly re-run to pick up new contacts.

## Output format

```
Birthday sync - <date> · Contacts scanned: <n>, with birthdays: <b>
Existing coverage: built-in Birthdays calendar <on/off>; <k> manual birthday events found

| # | Action | Contact | Date | Recurs | Reminder | Note |
|---|--------|---------|------|--------|----------|------|
| 1 | ADD  | Ana Ruiz | Mar 14 | yearly | on day + 3d before | close-contacts group |
| 2 | FIX  | Sam Lee  | Jul 2 (calendar says Jul 3) | yearly | on day | date mismatch |
| 3 | SKIP | Priya N. | Nov 30 | already synced | - | - |

Leap-day birthdays: <list + chosen rule>
Contacts with no birthday recorded (FYI): <count>

Say `apply`, `apply all`, or `apply 1,3` to create/update on <calendar name>.
--- after apply ---
Created <n>, fixed <m>. Nothing deleted.
```

## Do

- Verify @google-calendar and @google-contacts connectors and discover exact tool names first
- Check for the built-in Birthdays calendar and existing manual events before proposing - duplicates are this skill's main failure mode
- Make every event all-day and yearly-recurring; a one-off birthday event is a bug
- Handle missing birth years and Feb 29 explicitly rather than silently guessing
- Ask at most ONE clarifying question (usually: which calendar, and show ages or not?)

## Don't

- Don't add attendees or email anyone - birthday events are private reminders
- Don't display ages unless the user opted in
- Don't delete or overwrite existing birthday events without listing them as FIX rows and getting `apply`
- Don't create timed events - birthdays are all-day; a "9:00-10:00 birthday" clutters the day view
- Don't sync contacts the user excluded (e.g. business contacts) just because they have a birthday field

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Output matches requested tone and channel
- [ ] No placeholder brackets left unfilled (use sensible defaults or ask)
- [ ] User can copy-paste the result immediately
- [ ] Required integrations (@google-calendar, @google-contacts) verified in your AI tool's connector settings before `use_tool` calls
- [ ] Connector actions proposed, not auto-applied (unless read-only)

## Schedule

Recommended cadence: **monthly**

Example: `/loop 7d Run /gcal-birthday-sync`
```

# /gcal-family-merge

Unified view of work and personal calendars.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

The household runs on several calendars - user's work calendar, personal calendar, partner's shared calendar, kids' school/activities calendar - and nobody sees the whole picture. Run before the week starts (Sunday evening works well) to get one merged week view that surfaces collisions like "offsite Tuesday overlaps school pickup" and unclaimed family logistics.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; only ask if missing:

- Which calendars to merge (list what the account can see and let the user pick; shared partner/kids calendars must already be shared with this account)
- Week to view (default: next 7 days, Mon-Sun)
- Family logistics that recur but may not be on any calendar (school pickup time, practice runs) to check coverage against
- Timezones in play, if any family member or trip crosses zones

## Tone & voice

- **Tone:** Time-zone aware. Propose calendar changes before applying.
- **Audience:** Infer from user context.

## Connector access (required)

This skill **cannot run from text alone** - it must call live integrations.
Before any workflow step:

1. Check your AI tool's connector settings and confirm each integration below is enabled and authenticated (Grok: run `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: enable the app in settings · other tools: ask your AI what app connections it has).
2. Use `search_tool` to discover tools for each connector (search by app name).
3. Call integrations with `use_tool` and fully-qualified names (`server__tool_name`).
4. Connector tool names vary by AI tool. Discover the available tools first, then call the exact tool name exposed in the current session.

### Required integrations

- **@google-calendar** - Google Calendar
  - MCP server key: `google-calendar`
  - Example tools: `google-calendar__list_events`, `google-calendar__create_event`, `google-calendar__suggest_time`

### If a connector is missing

Stop immediately. Tell the user:

```
This skill needs <@mention> enabled.
1. Open your AI tool's connector/integration settings (Grok: `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: app settings)
2. Enable and authenticate <app>
3. Refresh your tool list, then run this skill again
```

Do **not** pretend you accessed Gmail/Canva/Drive without a successful `use_tool` call.

## Safety

- The merged view is **read-only** - no events are created, moved, or declined by this skill.
- If the user wants a conflict fixed (move an event, add a pickup block), propose the specific change in a review table and wait for `apply`. Never modify events on a partner's or child's calendar without flagging that the change is on *someone else's* calendar.

## Workflow

1. Discover the Calendar tools, then list the calendars visible to this account. Confirm with the user which ones are "the family set" - a missing shared calendar makes the merge silently wrong, so name what you're merging.
2. Pull all events for the chosen week from every selected calendar, keeping the source calendar on each event. Include all-day events (school holidays, trips) - they change everything and are easy to drop.
3. Normalize times to the household's home timezone; tag any event whose original timezone differs (e.g. a work call set in another office's zone).
4. Detect collisions, in three flavors:
   - **Hard conflict**: two events needing the same person at the same time (work meeting vs school pickup).
   - **Coverage gap**: a kid/household commitment with no adult unclaimed against the other calendars ("who takes Wednesday practice?").
   - **Crunch**: back-to-back events at different locations with no travel time between calendars.
5. Note load imbalance across the week only as observation ("Thu has 4 family commitments, Tue none") - useful for planning, not a judgment.
6. Present the merged week; offer fixes (move / block time / assign) as an `apply`-gated proposal list only if the user asks.

## Output format

```
Family week - <Mon date> to <Sun date> · merged: <calendar names>

⚠️ CONFLICTS
| When | Collision | Calendars involved | Who's double-booked |

❓ UNCLAIMED
- Wed 17:00 football practice (kids cal) - no adult blocked on either work/personal cal

MERGED WEEK
Mon <date>
  all-day: <event> [cal]
  09:00-09:30 <event> [work]
  15:30 school pickup [kids]
Tue <date>
  ...
(one block per day; every event tagged with its source calendar)

Timezone notes: <events not in home tz, or "none">
Want any fixes proposed (move/block/assign)? I'll show them for approval first.
```

## Do

- Verify the @google-calendar connector and discover exact tool names first
- Name the calendars actually merged at the top - an incomplete merge presented as complete causes missed pickups
- Keep the source-calendar tag on every line; "who owns this event" is the whole point
- Include all-day events and multi-day trips in conflict detection
- Ask at most ONE clarifying question (usually: which calendars are in the family set?)

## Don't

- Don't write, move, decline, or RSVP to anything - this is a view; changes need their own `apply`-gated proposal
- Don't propose edits to a partner's or child's calendar as if it were the user's own - always flag whose calendar a fix touches
- Don't drop events marked private/busy-only; show them as "busy" blocks so conflicts still surface
- Don't collapse timezones silently - a 9:00 PST call is 12:00 for an EST household
- Don't treat overlapping *all-day* events (two holidays) as hard conflicts

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Output matches requested tone and channel
- [ ] No placeholder brackets left unfilled (use sensible defaults or ask)
- [ ] User can copy-paste the result immediately
- [ ] Required integrations (@google-calendar) verified in your AI tool's connector settings before `use_tool` calls
- [ ] Connector actions proposed, not auto-applied (unless read-only)

## Schedule

Recommended cadence: **weekly**

Example: `/loop 7d Run /gcal-family-merge`
```

## When to use

Walking into meetings cold: the user's day is stacked and they want a one-page brief per meeting - who's attending, why it's happening, what was last said over email, and which docs to have open - assembled from Calendar, Gmail, and Drive before the day starts. Run each morning, or on demand before a single important meeting.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; only ask if missing:

- Which meetings: all of today's (default), tomorrow's, or one named meeting
- Skip list: solo blocks, focus time, and large all-hands are usually noise - confirm what to exclude
- Depth: quick brief (attendees + agenda + last email) vs deep prep (also related Drive docs and open threads)
- Anything the user specifically wants to walk in knowing (e.g. "status of the invoice dispute" before the client call)

## Tone & voice

- **Tone:** Time-zone aware. Propose calendar changes before applying.

```

3. Refresh your tool list, then run this skill again
```

Do **not** pretend you accessed Gmail/Canva/Drive without a successful `use_tool` call.

## Safety

- Prep is **read-only** across Calendar, Gmail, and Drive - no RSVPs changed, no mail sent, no docs edited.

3. Pull email context per meeting from Gmail: search threads with the organizer and key attendees over the last ~30 days plus threads matching distinctive title keywords (`from:organizer OR subject:"<project>" newer_than:30d`). Extract only what matters: the last substantive exchange, open questions aimed at the user, and commitments made ("you promised the numbers by this meeting").
4. Pull documents: files attached to the invite first, then Drive search on title keywords and files recently shared by attendees. Prefer docs modified in the last 2 weeks; link, don't summarize, unless deep prep was requested.
5. Detect prep-relevant red flags: agenda-less recurring meeting (suggest asking for one), key attendee declined, back-to-back with the previous meeting in a different location/call, and anything the user promised to deliver *for* this meeting that has no evidence of being done.
6. Assemble one brief per meeting, ordered by start time, sized to be read in under a minute each.

```
Meeting prep - <day, date> · <n> meetings (skipped: <m> solo/all-hands)

Why now: <agenda line or inferred purpose>

You owe them: <commitment or "nothing outstanding">
They owe you: <or "-">
Docs to have open: <title → link> (updated <date>)
Watch out: <red flag or "-">

━━ 11:00-12:00 · <next meeting>
...

Day-level notes: no agenda on 2 recurring meetings · 14:00 and 14:30 are back-to-back across offices

```

- Read the invite body and attachments before searching anywhere else

- Keep each brief scannable in under a minute; link docs instead of retelling them
- State it plainly when no context exists ("no email history with these attendees") rather than padding

## Don't

- Don't change RSVPs, send mail, or edit docs while prepping
- Don't summarize a Drive doc you couldn't actually open - link it and say so
- Don't dump whole email threads into the brief; two lines of the latest substance beats a transcript
- Don't prep skipped meetings or invent agendas for agenda-less ones - flag the gap instead
- Don't mix up meetings with similar names (weekly vs monthly "Product sync") - anchor every search to attendees + date

# /gcal-suggest-time

Find meeting times with Google Calendar suggest_time.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

"Find me a time with Priya and Tom next week for 45 minutes" - the user needs concrete meeting-time options that respect everyone's availability, working hours, and timezones, presented as ranked choices they can copy into an email or turn into an invite after approval.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; only ask if missing:

- Attendees (emails - free/busy lookup needs addresses, not just first names) and whose attendance is required vs optional
- Duration and how soon ("this week", "next 2 weeks", a hard deadline)
- Constraints: user's no-go zones (before 9, lunch, Fridays), preference for morning/afternoon
- Attendee timezones - ask whenever any attendee is in another region; a "perfect" slot at their 6am is not a slot

## Tone & voice

- **Tone:** Time-zone aware. Propose calendar changes before applying.
- **Audience:** Infer from user context.

## Connector access (required)

This skill **cannot run from text alone** - it must call live integrations.
Before any workflow step:

1. Check your AI tool's connector settings and confirm each integration below is enabled and authenticated (Grok: run `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: enable the app in settings · other tools: ask your AI what app connections it has).
2. Use `search_tool` to discover tools for each connector (search by app name).
3. Call integrations with `use_tool` and fully-qualified names (`server__tool_name`).
4. Connector tool names vary by AI tool. Discover the available tools first, then call the exact tool name exposed in the current session.

### Required integrations

- **@google-calendar** - Google Calendar
  - MCP server key: `google-calendar`
  - Example tools: `google-calendar__list_events`, `google-calendar__create_event`, `google-calendar__suggest_time`

### If a connector is missing

Stop immediately. Tell the user:

```
This skill needs <@mention> enabled.
1. Open your AI tool's connector/integration settings (Grok: `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: app settings)
2. Enable and authenticate <app>
3. Refresh your tool list, then run this skill again
```

Do **not** pretend you accessed Gmail/Canva/Drive without a successful `use_tool` call.

## Safety

- Finding times is read-only. **Creating the invite is a write**: show the final event (title, time, attendee list) and wait for `apply` - sending an invite emails every attendee instantly.
- Never book on behalf of other attendees' calendars.

## Workflow

1. Discover the Calendar tools in this session. If a suggest-time/free-busy tool exists (e.g. `google-calendar__suggest_time` - verify the exact name), use it with attendees, duration, and window. If only event-listing exists, fall back to reading the user's calendar and computing their open slots, and be explicit that other attendees' availability was NOT checked.
2. Note the limits of visibility: external attendees' free/busy is often hidden. Every option must state whose calendars were actually checked - a slot that only clears the user's calendar is a *candidate*, not a confirmed fit.
3. Apply constraint filtering to raw availability:
   - Working hours in **each attendee's own timezone**; show cross-zone options in both zones.
   - The user's no-go zones and existing soft blocks (focus time - treat as busy unless the user says otherwise).
   - Avoid creating back-to-backs: prefer slots with ≥15 min gap on both sides for the user.
4. Rank the surviving slots: earliest inside everyone's comfortable hours first; midday overlap wins for cross-zone groups; penalize Friday-late and Monday-early edges.
5. Offer 3-5 options. If nothing clears every filter, show the least-bad options with the violated constraint named ("works, but it's 8:00 for Tom") - and say which required attendee blocks everything.
6. On the user's pick: draft the invite (title, agenda line, conferencing link preference, attendees) and create it only after `apply`.

## Output format

```
Time search - <duration> with <attendees>, window <range>
Checked: your calendar + free/busy for <who> (not visible: <who> - treat as unconfirmed)

| # | Option (your tz) | Their local time(s) | Fit notes |
|---|------------------|---------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Tue Jul 14, 10:00-10:45 IST | 15:30 AEST (Priya) | all clear; gaps both sides |
| 2 | Wed Jul 15, 16:00-16:45 IST | 21:30 AEST | ⚠ evening for Priya |

Recommendation: #1 - <one-line reason>
Copy-paste for your email: "Would Tue Jul 14 at 10:00 IST / 15:30 AEST work? Backup: ..."

Say `book 1` and I'll show the full invite for approval before anything is sent.
```

## Do

- Verify the @google-calendar connector and discover the exact tool names first - suggest-time tools vary widely between AI tools
- State plainly whose availability was actually checked; never present a user-only slot as "everyone is free"
- Show every cross-timezone option in each participant's local time
- Give a copy-paste sentence with the top option and a backup, since many scheduling talks happen over email anyway
- Ask at most ONE clarifying question (usually: attendee emails or timezones)

## Don't

- Don't send an invite without the `apply`-gated review - invites email people immediately
- Don't schedule over focus blocks or tentative events without flagging it
- Don't assume attendees share the user's timezone or workweek (weekends differ by country)
- Don't offer slots you computed from a stale window - re-check availability if the conversation resumed hours later
- Don't fabricate other people's availability when free/busy is hidden - mark those options unconfirmed

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Output matches requested tone and channel
- [ ] No placeholder brackets left unfilled (use sensible defaults or ask)
- [ ] User can copy-paste the result immediately
- [ ] Required integrations (@google-calendar) verified in your AI tool's connector settings before `use_tool` calls
- [ ] Connector actions proposed, not auto-applied (unless read-only)
```

---
name: gcal-travel-buffers
description: >
  Add travel buffer blocks around flights. Requires integrations: @google-calendar. Uses search_tool + use_tool. Cadence: on-demand. Triggers: "gcal travel buffers", /gcal-travel-buffers.
metadata:
  category: google-calendar

# /gcal-travel-buffers

Add travel buffer blocks around flights.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A trip is on the calendar as a bare flight event, which means the calendar lies: it shows the user "free" during airport transit, and colleagues book meetings 30 minutes before boarding. Run this after booking travel to wrap each flight (or train) with leave-for-airport, transit, and landing-recovery blocks so the whole travel day reads as busy.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; only ask if missing:

- Which trip(s): scan the next 60 days for flight-like events, or a specific named trip
- Home-to-airport travel time, and whether checked bags / international check-in applies (drives the pre-flight buffer: ~2h domestic, ~3h international by default)
- Recovery preference after landing: transit-home block only, or also a no-meetings recovery block (long-haul/red-eye default: rest of the arrival day)
- Whether flights sit on the user's own calendar or a shared/travel calendar

## Tone & voice

- **Tone:** Time-zone aware. Propose calendar changes before applying.
- **Audience:** Infer from user context.

## Connector access (required)

This skill **cannot run from text alone** - it must call live integrations.
Before any workflow step:

1. Check your AI tool's connector settings and confirm each integration below is enabled and authenticated (Grok: run `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: enable the app in settings · other tools: ask your AI what app connections it has).
2. Use `search_tool` to discover tools for each connector (search by app name).
3. Call integrations with `use_tool` and fully-qualified names (`server__tool_name`).
4. Connector tool names vary by AI tool. Discover the available tools first, then call the exact tool name exposed in the current session.

### Required integrations

- **@google-calendar** - Google Calendar
  - MCP server key: `google-calendar`
  - Example tools: `google-calendar__list_events`, `google-calendar__create_event`, `google-calendar__suggest_time`

### If a connector is missing

Stop immediately. Tell the user:

```
This skill needs <@mention> enabled.
1. Open your AI tool's connector/integration settings (Grok: `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: app settings)
2. Enable and authenticate <app>
3. Refresh your tool list, then run this skill again
```

Do **not** pretend you accessed Gmail/Canva/Drive without a successful `use_tool` call.

## Safety

- **Propose before write**: show a review table; wait for user to say `apply`, `apply all`, or `apply 1,3`.
- Never delete, send, or publish without explicit confirmation.
- Summarize every action taken at the end of the run so the user can review it.

## Workflow

1. Discover the Calendar tools, then find travel events in the window: search titles for flight patterns (airline codes like "UA 22", "Flight", airport pairs "SFO → JFK", "✈") across the user's calendars, including any auto-added "Events from Gmail" flight entries.
2. For each flight, read departure and arrival times **with their stated timezones** - departure is in origin-airport local time, arrival in destination local time. All buffer math must be done per-zone; never assume one timezone across a flight.
3. Compute the buffer set per flight:
   - **Leave for airport**: departure − (check-in lead: ~2h domestic / ~3h international, per user's input) − door-to-airport travel time.
   - **Airport + flight** is already covered by the flight event itself; extend if the flight event only covers wheels-up to wheels-down.
   - **Arrival transit**: landing + deplane/bags/immigration (30-90 min by trip type) + transfer to hotel/home.
   - **Recovery** (optional): long-haul or red-eye → block the rest of the arrival day as no-meetings.
4. Check each proposed buffer against existing events. A meeting sitting inside a buffer window is exactly what this skill exists to catch - list it as a conflict with a suggested action (decline/move), but do not touch it.
5. Present the review table (one row per proposed block, grouped by flight). Create only after `apply`, `apply all`, or `apply 1,3`, as **busy, no-guest** events named clearly ("Leave for SFO - UA 22").
6. Report created blocks and any conflicts left for the user to resolve.

## Output format

```
Travel buffers - <n> flights found in the next <window>

✈ UA 22 · SFO → JFK · Tue Jul 21, dep 08:15 PDT, arr 16:45 EDT
| # | Proposed block | Local time | Length | Conflicts with |
|---|----------------|-----------|--------|----------------|
| 1 | Leave for SFO | 05:45-06:15 PDT | 30m | - |
| 2 | Check-in & security | 06:15-08:15 PDT | 2h | 07:30 standup ⚠ |
| 3 | Arrival transit (JFK → hotel) | 16:45-18:00 EDT | 1h15 | - |
| 4 | Recovery - no meetings | 18:00-EOD EDT | - | 19:00 dinner (keep?) |

Conflicts needing your call: #2 overlaps daily standup - decline it, or move buffer?
Say `apply`, `apply all`, or `apply 1,3`. Blocks are created as Busy with no guests.
--- after apply ---
Created <n> blocks across <f> flights. Untouched conflicts: <list>.
```

## Do

- Verify the @google-calendar connector and discover exact tool names first
- Do all time math in the airport-local timezone on each side of the flight; label every block with its zone
- Scale pre-flight buffers by trip type (international, checked bags, unfamiliar airport → longer)
- Name blocks so a colleague reading "Busy" understands nothing, but the user reading the title knows exactly what it is
- Flag meetings inside buffer windows as explicit conflicts to resolve

## Don't

- Don't average or convert timezones across a flight - an 08:15 PDT departure has nothing to do with EDT
- Don't decline, move, or delete conflicting meetings yourself - surface them and wait for the user's decision
- Don't invite anyone to buffer blocks or copy them to shared calendars uninvited
- Don't create buffers for past flights or duplicates on a re-run - check for existing "Leave for" blocks first
- Don't skip the arrival side; landing buffers are forgotten more often than departure ones

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Output matches requested tone and channel
- [ ] No placeholder brackets left unfilled (use sensible defaults or ask)
- [ ] User can copy-paste the result immediately
- [ ] Required integrations (@google-calendar) verified in your AI tool's connector settings before `use_tool` calls
- [ ] Connector actions proposed, not auto-applied (unless read-only)
```

# /gcal-week-audit

Audit Google Calendar for conflicts and gaps.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Sunday-evening or Monday-morning sanity check on the week ahead: find double-bookings, missing RSVPs, meetings with no agenda or location, zero-gap meeting chains, and days with no lunch or focus time - before the week starts and while there's still room to fix it.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; only ask if missing:

- Window (default: next 7 days, Mon-Sun) and which calendars count (work + personal recommended)
- The user's working hours and non-negotiables (lunch window, hard stop time)
- Buffer standard: minimum gap the user wants between meetings (default: 15 min)
- What "too many meetings" means for them, if they have a threshold (e.g. >5h/day)

## Tone & voice

- **Tone:** Time-zone aware. Propose calendar changes before applying.
- **Audience:** Infer from user context.

## Connector access (required)

This skill **cannot run from text alone** - it must call live integrations.
Before any workflow step:

1. Check your AI tool's connector settings and confirm each integration below is enabled and authenticated (Grok: run `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: enable the app in settings · other tools: ask your AI what app connections it has).
2. Use `search_tool` to discover tools for each connector (search by app name).
3. Call integrations with `use_tool` and fully-qualified names (`server__tool_name`).
4. Connector tool names vary by AI tool. Discover the available tools first, then call the exact tool name exposed in the current session.

### Required integrations

- **@google-calendar** - Google Calendar
  - MCP server key: `google-calendar`
  - Example tools: `google-calendar__list_events`, `google-calendar__create_event`, `google-calendar__suggest_time`

### If a connector is missing

Stop immediately. Tell the user:

```
This skill needs <@mention> enabled.
1. Open your AI tool's connector/integration settings (Grok: `/mcps` · Claude: Settings → Connectors · Cursor: Settings → MCP · ChatGPT: app settings)
2. Enable and authenticate <app>
3. Refresh your tool list, then run this skill again
```

Do **not** pretend you accessed Gmail/Canva/Drive without a successful `use_tool` call.

## Safety

- The audit itself is **read-only**. Any fix (decline, move, shorten, add a buffer) is proposed in a review table and executed only after `apply`, `apply all`, or `apply 1,3`.
- Never decline or move a meeting with other attendees without warning that they will be notified.

## Workflow

1. Discover the Calendar tools, then pull all events for the window from every in-scope calendar - conflicts between work and personal calendars are the ones Google itself won't show. Normalize to the user's timezone; keep original zones on cross-zone events.
2. Run the conflict pass:
   - **Hard overlaps**: two accepted events sharing minutes. Note which one has more attendees/seniority as the likely keeper, but decide nothing.
   - **Unanswered invites**: events sitting at "needs RSVP" - silent maybes that may or may not block time.
   - **Phantom recurrences**: recurring meetings where the organizer left or the description says "canceled" but the series lives on.
3. Run the hygiene pass per meeting: missing agenda/description, missing location or conferencing link, guest-list oddities (user is optional yet attends every week), meetings that regularly run over into the next one.
4. Run the structure pass per day: chains of 3+ back-to-backs with no gap ≥ the buffer standard, no lunch window, meetings outside working hours, total meeting hours vs the user's threshold, and whether any focus time survived.
5. For each finding, attach one concrete suggested fix (decline X, shorten Y to 25 min, ask organizer of Z for an agenda, add a lunch block Thursday). Group fixes into an `apply`-gated list; declines/moves that notify others get an explicit warning tag.
6. End with a two-line overall read of the week ("Tuesday is the crunch day; Friday afternoon is your only deep-work window").

## Output format

```
Week audit - <Mon date>-<Sun date> · calendars: <list>
Meeting load: <h> hours across <n> meetings · heaviest day: <day, h>

🔴 CONFLICTS (<n>)
| When | Events colliding | Suggested resolution |

🟡 NEEDS RSVP (<n>)
| Event | When | Organizer |

🧹 HYGIENE (<n>)
- Wed 10:00 "Sync" - recurring, no agenda for 6 weeks → ask organizer or propose skip
- Thu 15:00 client call - no meet link

🏗 STRUCTURE
- Tue: 5 back-to-backs 09:00-14:00, no lunch → suggest 25-min meetings or a 12:30 block
- Focus time surviving this week: <h> hours

Fixes I can make for you:
| # | Fix | Notifies others? |
Say `apply 1,3` to execute. Everything else is yours to handle.

Bottom line: <2-line read of the week>
```

## Do

- Verify the @google-calendar connector and discover exact tool names first
- Audit across ALL of the user's calendars - the classic missed conflict is dentist (personal) vs standup (work)
- Distinguish accepted, tentative, and needs-RSVP events; only accepted ones make hard conflicts
- Attach a concrete, one-line fix to every finding - an audit without proposed actions is just bad news
- Ask at most ONE clarifying question (usually: working hours / buffer standard)

## Don't

- Don't auto-decline, move, or shorten anything - every fix goes through the `apply` gate
- Don't propose changes to a meeting without checking the invitee list; declining a 20-person meeting differs from moving a 1:1
- Don't count all-day items (holidays, OOO markers) as meeting load
- Don't flag deliberate patterns as problems twice - if the user says back-to-backs on Monday are intentional, drop it from future audits in this session
- Don't ignore timezone-shifted recurring meetings around DST changes - they are a top source of silent conflicts

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Output matches requested tone and channel
- [ ] No placeholder brackets left unfilled (use sensible defaults or ask)
- [ ] User can copy-paste the result immediately
- [ ] Required integrations (@google-calendar) verified in your AI tool's connector settings before `use_tool` calls
- [ ] Connector actions proposed, not auto-applied (unless read-only)

## Schedule

Recommended cadence: **weekly**

Example: `/loop 7d Run /gcal-week-audit`
```
